gh-w5-f1.answer:
ArrayList — the most-used List in Java. Backed by a dynamic array: fast random access by index, slower insert/delete in the middle.

Requirements:
1. Create an ArrayList<String> for student names and add at least 5 entries with add().
2. Demonstrate get(index), set(index, value), remove(index), and size().
3. Print the list with a for-each loop and with a classic for loop using get().
4. Sort the list with Collections.sort() and print again.
5. Search for an element with contains() and indexOf().

Key concepts:
• List allows duplicates and maintains insertion order.
• ArrayList grows automatically (roughly 1.5× capacity when full).
• Use generics: ArrayList<String>, not raw ArrayList.
• Prefer ArrayList when you read by index often; use LinkedList when you insert/delete at ends frequently.
gh-w5-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: REST APIs return JSON arrays that become ArrayList in Java; shopping carts, todo lists, and search results all use List. ArrayList is the default choice in Spring Boot services and Hibernate collections.

Compile & run:
javac ArrayListExamples.java
java ArrayListExamples

Expected: original list, sorted list, and search results printed clearly.

Push to java-week-5-collections:
git add ArrayListExamples.java
git commit -m "Add ArrayListExamples.java — List CRUD and sorting"
git push

Common mistake: list.get(10) when size is 5 throws IndexOutOfBoundsException — always check index < size().

gh-w5-f2.answer:
LinkedList — a doubly linked list implementation of List and Deque. Each element points to the next and previous node.

Requirements:
1. Create a LinkedList<Integer> and add elements at the end with add() and at the beginning with addFirst().
2. Remove from front (removeFirst) and back (removeLast).
3. Use add(index, element) to insert in the middle.
4. Traverse with Iterator and with for-each.
5. Compare behavior: note that get(index) on LinkedList is O(n) — avoid random access in loops.

Key concepts:
• LinkedList implements both List and Deque — can be used as a queue or stack.
• No shifting of entire array on insert at ends — O(1) addFirst/addLast.
• Memory overhead: each node stores data + two references.
• Use when frequent insert/delete at both ends; avoid when you need fast get(i).
gh-w5-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: Browser back/forward history, undo stacks, and job queues often use linked structures. LinkedList also backs some Deque use cases before ArrayDeque became preferred for pure queue/stack work.

Compile & run:
javac LinkedListExamples.java
java LinkedListExamples

Push:
git add LinkedListExamples.java
git commit -m "Add LinkedListExamples.java — insert at ends and traverse"
git push

Tip: For a simple FIFO queue today, PriorityQueue or ArrayDeque is often better — but LinkedList teaches how nodes and pointers work.

gh-w5-f3.answer:
HashSet — stores unique elements with no guaranteed order. Uses hash table internally; average O(1) add, remove, contains.

Requirements:
1. Create a HashSet<String> and add at least 6 strings including one duplicate — prove duplicates are ignored.
2. Demonstrate add(), remove(), contains(), size(), and isEmpty().
3. Convert a List with duplicates to a Set to remove duplicates.
4. Iterate with enhanced for-loop and forEach (lambda).
5. Optional: show LinkedHashSet (insertion order) or TreeSet (sorted) in comments for comparison.

Key concepts:
• Set contract: no duplicate elements (equals/hashCode matter for custom objects).
• HashSet does not maintain insertion order — order may change between runs.
• null is allowed once in HashSet.
• For custom classes, override equals() and hashCode() or duplicates won't be detected.
gh-w5-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Unique email registration, distinct user IDs in analytics, tag deduplication, and "already visited" tracking in crawlers all use Set. Interviewers frequently ask HashSet vs TreeSet.

Compile & run:
javac HashSetExamples.java
java HashSetExamples

Push:
git add HashSetExamples.java
git commit -m "Add HashSetExamples.java — unique elements and dedup"
git push

Watch out: two Student objects with same roll number but default equals() are treated as different — override equals/hashCode for domain objects.

gh-w5-f4.answer:
HashMap — key-value pairs with fast lookup by key. Keys must be unique; values can repeat.

Requirements:
1. Create HashMap<String, Integer> mapping student names to marks (at least 5 entries).
2. Use put(), get(), containsKey(), containsValue(), remove(), and size().
3. Update a score with put() on an existing key.
4. Iterate using entrySet() — print each key and value.
5. Build a word-frequency counter: split a sentence and count occurrences with getOrDefault().

Key concepts:
• Map is not part of Collection interface — separate hierarchy.
• HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values.
• get(key) returns null if missing — use getOrDefault(key, default) for safety.
• Internal: buckets + hash function; Java 8+ uses tree for long collision chains.
gh-w5-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Caches (key → value), session storage, product catalogs (SKU → price), and configuration maps are everywhere in backend Java. Spring's @Autowired bean maps and JSON deserialization to Map<String, Object> rely on this pattern.

Compile & run:
javac HashMapExamples.java
java HashMapExamples

Push:
git add HashMapExamples.java
git commit -m "Add HashMapExamples.java — key-value ops and frequency"
git push

Interview tip: Know difference from Hashtable (legacy, synchronized, no null keys) and TreeMap (sorted keys, O(log n)).

gh-w5-f5.answer:
Queue — FIFO (First In, First Out) processing. Java provides Queue interface with LinkedList, PriorityQueue, and ArrayDeque implementations.

Requirements:
1. Use Queue<String> with LinkedList — offer() to enqueue, poll() to dequeue, peek() to view front without removing.
2. Simulate a ticket counter: add 5 customers, serve them one by one with poll().
3. Create PriorityQueue<Integer> with natural ordering — poll returns smallest first.
4. Use ArrayDeque as a stack: push() and pop() for LIFO demo.
5. Handle empty queue: poll() returns null; peek() returns null — check before use.

Key concepts:
• offer/add enqueue; poll/remove dequeue; peek/element view head.
• PriorityQueue: elements ordered by priority (Comparable or Comparator).
• ArrayDeque: faster than Stack class (legacy) for stack operations.
• Queue fits waiting lines, task scheduling, BFS graph traversal.
gh-w5-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ), thread pool work queues, print spoolers, and hospital triage all model waiting work as queues. PriorityQueue powers Dijkstra's algorithm and job schedulers.

Compile & run:
javac QueueExamples.java
java QueueExamples

Push:
git add QueueExamples.java
git commit -m "Add QueueExamples.java — FIFO, priority, and stack"
git push

Note: LinkedList implements Queue; for most queue-only use cases, ArrayDeque is faster — but both are valid for learning.

gh-w5-f6.answer:
Comparable vs Comparator — two ways to sort custom objects.

Requirements:
1. Create a Student class with name, rollNumber, and marks.
2. Implement Comparable<Student> — compareTo by rollNumber (natural order).
3. Sort a List<Student> with Collections.sort(list) using natural order.
4. Sort by marks descending using Comparator.comparingInt(Student::getMarks).reversed().
5. Sort by name alphabetically with a lambda Comparator: (a, b) -> a.getName().compareTo(b.getName()).
6. Use Collections.max() / min() with a Comparator.

Key concepts:
• Comparable: inside the class, one natural ordering via compareTo().
• Comparator: external, multiple sort orders without changing the class.
• compareTo returns negative/zero/positive — same contract as Comparator.compare().
• Method references (Student::getMarks) work with Comparator.comparing().
gh-w5-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: E-commerce "sort by price", HR "sort by salary", and leaderboard rankings all need flexible sorting. Hibernate and JPA queries often mirror Comparator logic in ORDER BY clauses.

Compile & run:
javac ComparatorExamples.java Student.java
java ComparatorExamples

(If Student is inner class or same file, adjust compile command.)

Push:
git add ComparatorExamples.java
git commit -m "Add ComparatorExamples.java — Comparable and custom sort"
git push

Common error: ClassCastException when sorting objects that don't implement Comparable and you call sort() without passing a Comparator.

gh-w5-f7.answer:
Student Management System — a console CRUD app using ArrayList and Comparator. This is your Week 5 capstone project.

Requirements:
1. Student model: rollNumber (int, unique), name, branch, marks.
2. Store students in ArrayList<Student>.
3. Menu-driven console: 1) Add  2) Remove by roll  3) Search by roll  4) Display all  5) Sort by marks  6) Exit.
4. Add: reject duplicate roll numbers.
5. Sort: use Comparator.comparingInt by marks (highest first).
6. Search: linear scan or stream filter — return "Not found" if missing.

Key concepts:
• In-memory CRUD before JDBC — same operations, List instead of database.
• Validation at add time prevents bad data.
• Scanner for menu input; loop until exit.
• Separate display logic from collection manipulation for readability.
gh-w5-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: Every admin panel — students, employees, products — is CRUD. This project proves you can model entities, enforce uniqueness, and sort/search collections — skills directly transferred to Spring Boot + JPA next month.

Compile & run:
javac StudentManagementSystem.java
java StudentManagementSystem

Test: add 3 students, try duplicate roll, sort, search, remove.

Push:
git add StudentManagementSystem.java
git commit -m "Add StudentManagementSystem.java — ArrayList CRUD project"
git push

gh-w5-f8.answer:
Shopping Cart — e-commerce cart using List for line items and HashMap for product catalog.

Requirements:
1. Product catalog: HashMap<String, Product> keyed by productId (id, name, price).
2. Cart: List<CartItem> where CartItem holds productId, quantity.
3. Operations: addToCart(id, qty), removeFromCart(id), updateQuantity(id, qty), displayCart(), calculateTotal().
4. Validate: product must exist in catalog; quantity > 0.
5. Print formatted summary: item name, unit price, qty, line total, grand total.

Key concepts:
• Map for lookup (O(1) by id); List for ordered cart items.
• Combining structures: catalog is reference data, cart is session data.
• BigDecimal is better for money in production — double is OK for learning.
• Empty cart should print a friendly message, not crash.
gh-w5-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Amazon, Flipkart, and every checkout flow use cart + catalog. Backend services store catalog in Redis/DB and cart in session or DB — same logical split you build here.

Compile & run:
javac ShoppingCart.java
java ShoppingCart

Push:
git add ShoppingCart.java
git commit -m "Add ShoppingCart.java — List cart and Map catalog"
git push

gh-w5-f9.answer:
Employee Directory — lookup and filter employees using HashMap and ArrayList together.

Requirements:
1. Employee: id (String), name, department, salary.
2. HashMap<String, Employee> for O(1) lookup by employee ID.
3. ArrayList<Employee> for listing and filtering.
4. Features: addEmployee, findById, filterByDepartment(dept), sortBySalary(), displayAll().
5. Seed at least 6 employees across 3 departments in main() or a setup method.
6. Print department filter results and top earner after salary sort.

Key concepts:
• Dual storage: Map for fast ID lookup, List for iteration and filters.
• Keep Map and List in sync on add/remove (remove from both).
• filterByDepartment: loop or stream filter on List.
• Real directories also index by department — could use Map<String, List<Employee>> as bonus.
gh-w5-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: HR systems, Slack user directories, and org charts need fast lookup by ID plus flexible listing. Enterprise apps often cache Map views of DB rows for performance.

Compile & run:
javac EmployeeDirectory.java
java EmployeeDirectory

Push:

git commit -m "Add EmployeeDirectory.java — HashMap lookup and filters"
git push

gh-w5-f10.answer:
README.md is the front page of your repository — document your Week 5 Collections work clearly.

Write a README that includes:
1. Title: # java-week-5-collections
2. Description — Java Collections Framework programs for Prathyu Academy Week 5.
3. Topics covered — ArrayList, LinkedList, HashSet, HashMap, Queue, Comparable/Comparator, mini projects.
4. How to run — JDK 17+, compile all: javac *.java, run examples individually.
5. File table — each .java file and what it demonstrates.
6. Collection cheat sheet — when to use List vs Set vs Map (one short table or bullet list).
7. What you learned — 3–5 sentences in your own words.
8. Optional: link to Prathyu Academy, your name, date.

Use Markdown: headings, bullets, `code` for commands, tables for file list.
gh-w5-f10.realWorld:
Why it matters: Interviewers skim GitHub READMEs before cloning code. A Week 5 repo with a collection comparison table shows you understand trade-offs — not just syntax. This is often the difference between "completed assignments" and "job-ready portfolio."

No compile step for README — preview in IDE or on GitHub after push.

Push:
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add README — Week 5 collections summary"
git push

After pushing, verify headings and code blocks render correctly on github.com.
gh-w5-f10.code:
# java-week-5-collections

Week 5 Java Collections Framework — programs for Prathyu Academy.

## Topics covered
- ArrayList and LinkedList (List interface)
- HashSet (unique elements)
- HashMap (key-value pairs)
- Queue, PriorityQueue, ArrayDeque
- Comparable and Comparator
- Mini projects: Student Management, Shopping Cart, Employee Directory

## How to run
Requires JDK 17+.

```bash
javac *.java
java ArrayListExamples
java StudentManagementSystem
```

## Files
| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| ArrayListExamples.java | List CRUD, sort, search |
| LinkedListExamples.java | Insert at ends, Iterator |
| HashSetExamples.java | Unique elements, dedup |
| HashMapExamples.java | Key-value, frequency count |
| QueueExamples.java | FIFO, priority, stack |
| ComparatorExamples.java | Comparable vs Comparator |
| StudentManagementSystem.java | CRUD console project |
| ShoppingCart.java | Map catalog + List cart |
| EmployeeDirectory.java | HashMap lookup + filters |

## When to use which collection
| Need | Use |
|------|-----|
| Ordered list, index access | ArrayList |
| Frequent insert/delete at ends | LinkedList |
| Unique items | HashSet |
| Key → value lookup | HashMap |
| Waiting line / FIFO | Queue |

## What I learned
This week I learned to choose the right collection for each problem instead of always using ArrayList. HashMap made lookups fast, HashSet removed duplicates, and Comparator let me sort objects in multiple ways.
